plan.setup_phase: Step 1: Open a direct mutual fund account on Kuvera, Groww, or MFU (don't use a regular account—go direct). Step 2: Transfer ₹18,000 monthly from your bank to this account. Step 3: Set up auto-SIPs in the 6 funds above (₹5040+₹3600+₹2160+₹3600+₹1440+₹2160 = ₹18k) on the same date every month. Step 4: Keep ₹24,000 (6 months rent) separate in a liquid fund or savings account for emergency
review_checkpoints: After 3 months: Check if all your SIPs are running monthly. Download statements. Match actual vs target allocations. Step 5: At 6 months: verify NAV updates are reflecting. Step 6: At 12 months: annual review of returns, portfolio drift, and goal progress. Calculate actual corpus growth vs projected returns

Rebalancing guidelines: Review once a year. If drift >5% from target, shift money back. For example, if your equity exposure rises to 80% due to good stock performance, move some money to the hybrid/gold buckets to get back to your 76% equity target. Annual tax review for gold/gilt holdings if needed. Your projected returns are: base 13%, bull 17%, bear 9%. Your rationale: You're 29 with 26 years ahead—time is your ally. Pure equity exposure (~76%) captures India's growth, while 20% multi-asset + gold cushions volatility. Since you've been investing every month without fail, consistency compounds. Your creative career gives you flexibility to adjust SIPs if income rises. No 80C deduction needed (new regime), so ELSS not included. All funds are direct plans—lowest fees. Post-tax: equity funds taxed at 12.5% LTCG over 1 year, gold at your marginal slab (assuming 10%), better than old 30% slab. Disclaimer: For research/educational purposes only, not certified financial advice

Rationale: 26 year horizon lets you ride volatility. Your ₹18k SIPs keep buying at lower prices. Stock markets can drop 20-30% in bad years; gold stabilizes; multi-asset adds buffer. Disclaimer: research/educational purposes only, not certified financial advice.
risks: Stock markets can drop 20-30% in a year. Your SIPs keep buying at lower prices (bonus positions). Don't panic sell. Rebalance annually. If equity drifts above 80%, move back. If below 70%, add. Review quarterly: compare your 3-year returns vs category benchmarks. If any fund underperforms for 2 straight quarters, switch to category peers. Emergency fund: keep separate, don't touch unless crisis. Final: This plan is for research/educational purposes only, not certified financial advice
For research/educational purposes only, not certified financial advice
plan.disclaimer: For research/educational purposes only. Not certified financial advice.

sip_step_up.description: Increase SIP by 10% every year to match salary growth

aps.reasoning: The plan consists entirely of actively managed funds for its equity sleeve (Large & Midcap, Midcap, Smallcap), resulting in a 0.0 passive instrument fraction. The strategy exhibits a strong buy-and-hold character with an annual review cycle, minimal transaction costs (pure direct plans), and a 26-year investment horizon, indicating high passive scores for turnover, cost efficiency, and time alignment. However, the fund selection relies on past performance and categorical labels rather than deep individual stock research (research score moderate-low). The portfolio activeness score is moderate (0.45) due to the heavy concentration in active equity funds despite the overall passive operational framework.

pqs.reasoning: Goal alignment: The plan strongly matches the 26-year horizon and moderate risk appetite, with an emergency fund mentioned. Diversification is good across large/mid/small caps, gold, and hybrids, though the two Conservative Hybrids are somewhat redundant. Risk-return appropriateness is high given the long horizon and SIP strategy. Internal consistency is excellent; numbers, rationale, and rebalancing rules align perfectly. Tax efficiency is very good: the plan correctly identifies the 'new_regime' status and omits ELSS, but it calls a Conservative Hybrid (slab-taxed) 'stability anchor' while ignoring that a Sliding Hybrid (equity-taxed) would serve the same purpose better for a high-effective-tax-risk investor, costing some points. Overall, a high-quality plan.
